topic.title: Advanced Search
topic.shortdesc: Along with simple searches, the Content Fusion search engine also supports advanced query syntax that allows you to create more complex, flexible, and useful queries to make the search more precise.

body.p: This topic describes the advanced search features that you can use when searching on the Task Search page or Workspace search view. A short reference is also available in the UI via the help icon next to the search field.

section.title: Phrase Search
section.p: A query consists of terms and operators. Terms can be either single terms (individual words) or phrases (multiple words surrounded by quotes).
When you enclose words in double quotation marks, the search returns results only when the exact phrase appears in the content.
Search for the exact phrase:

section.title: Field Search
section.p: You can search within specific fields by specifying the field name followed by a colon (:) and the search term.
Search for a title that exactly matches The Right Way:
section.note: The field specification applies only to the term it directly precedes. For example: title:Do it right will search title for Do and search the default text field for it and right.
section.p: The available fields are:

ul.li: text — Full text content of the document

section.title: Wildcard Search
section.p: You can use wildcard characters in individual terms to broaden a search. Wildcards are not supported within quoted phrases.
? – matches a single character
* – matches zero or more characters
Find terms like meet or meat:
Find terms like test, tests, testing:
section.note: A wildcard term cannot start with * or ?.

section.title: Regular Expression Search
section.p: You can use regular expressions to match patterns. A regular expression should be enclosed in forward slashes (/.../).
Match boat or moat:

section.title: Proximity Search
section.p: To find terms that occur within a defined number of words from each other, append a tilde (~) and a number after a quoted phrase.
Find instances where dita and webhelp occur within 10 words of each other.

section.title: Boosting
section.p: You can increase the relative importance of a term by adding a caret (^) with a numeric boost factor after the term.
This increases the relevance of documents containing dita.
section.note: The default boost factor is 1. Values must be positive and can be less than 1 (for example, 0.2).

section.title: Boolean Operators
section.p: Boolean operators combine terms logically. The supported operators are AND, OR, +, NOT, and -. Operators must be in ALL CAPS.
When no operator is specified between terms, OR is assumed.
Find documents containing either term:
AND – Documents must contain both terms:
+ – Term is required:
NOT – Exclude documents with the following term:
- – Another way to exclude results:

section.title: Field Grouping
section.p: Use parentheses to group multiple conditions within a field.
Find titles that contain both dita and content search.

section.title: Escaping Special Characters
section.p: To search for characters that are part of the query syntax (for example ( ) * ? :), escape those characters with a backslash (\).
